name: Docker
description: A platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in lightweight, portable containers. Standardizes application packaging and deployment across environments.
overview: Docker revolutionized software development by making containers mainstream — packaging applications with all their dependencies into standardized, lightweight, and portable units. Launched in 2013, Docker uses OS-level virtualization to deliver software in packages called containers. Unlike VMs, containers share the host OS kernel, making them much more efficient. Docker provides tooling for building images (Dockerfile), sharing them (Docker Hub/registries), composing multi-service applications (Docker Compose), and orchestrating clusters (Docker Swarm, Kubernetes). The Docker ecosystem has become essential for DevOps, microservices, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud-native development. Docker ensures consistency across development, testing, and production environments — eliminating the 'it works on my machine' problem. Major cloud providers support Docker containers natively.

learningPath.title: Docker Fundamentals
learningPath.description: Understand container concepts, Docker architecture, and basic commands for managing containers and images.
topics: What are containers? Containers vs Virtual Machines
Docker architecture: client, daemon, containerd, runc
Installing Docker Desktop (Windows/Mac) and Docker Engine (Linux)
Docker Hub — finding and pulling public images

docker stop, start, restart, rm: container lifecycle
docker exec: running commands in running containers
Interactive mode (-it), detached mode (-d), port mapping (-p)
Understanding container isolation: namespaces and cgroups
projects: Run Nginx container with port mapping
Explore Docker Hub and run various images

learningPath.title: Docker Images & Dockerfile

topics: FROM: choosing base images (Alpine, Slim, Distroless)
COPY vs ADD: adding files to images
ENV and ARG: build-time and runtime variables

Multi-stage builds: compile in one stage, run in another
Minimizing image size: Alpine base, cleaning apt caches, .dockerignore
docker build: building images, tagging, build context
docker commit: creating images from containers
USER: running as non-root for security
projects: Node.js app Dockerfile with multi-stage build

learningPath.title: Docker Volumes & Data Persistence
learningPath.description: Learn Docker storage options: volumes, bind mounts, tmpfs, and data management patterns.
topics: Volumes: managed by Docker, stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes

Bind mounts: mounting host directories into containers
tmpfs mounts: in-memory storage for temporary data

learningPath.title: Docker Networking

Bridge network: default, custom bridge, DNS resolution

learningPath.title: Docker Security & Best Practices

Image scanning: Trivy, Snyk, Docker Scout

Resource limits (CPU, memory, PIDs) to prevent DoS

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Docker image and a container?
interviewQuestions.answer: An image is a read-only template with instructions for creating containers — the blueprint. A container is a runnable instance of an image — the actual running process. Images are built with Dockerfile, stored in registries. Containers have writable layers (copy-on-write), can be started, stopped, moved, and deleted. Multiple containers can run from the same image.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain Docker multi-stage builds and their benefits.
interviewQuestions.answer: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements in a Dockerfile. Each stage can use different base images. Only files from the final stage end up in the final image. Benefits: dramatically smaller images (no build tools, SDKs, or temp files in production), separation of build and runtime environments, no need for multiple Dockerfiles. Example: compile Go in golang:alpine stage, copy binary to scratch stage.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Docker networking work? Explain bridge, host, and overlay.
interviewQuestions.answer: Bridge (default): containers on same bridge can communicate via IP, isolated from outside. Host: container shares host network stack, no isolation but better performance. Overlay: connects containers across multiple Docker hosts (Swarm mode). Bridge is for single-host container communication. Host for performance-critical apps needing direct network access. Overlay for multi-host microservices.

interviewQuestions.question: What is Docker Compose and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container applications using a YAML file. It creates networks, volumes, and starts all services with one command. Use for: local development environments (app + DB + cache), testing infrastructure, CI services, simple production deployments (single host). Don't use for: multi-host deployments, production at scale (use Swarm/Kubernetes).

interviewQuestions.question: How do you manage persistent data in Docker?
interviewQuestions.answer: Volumes: managed by Docker, stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes, backup/restore possible, recommended for persistent data. Bind mounts: mount host directory into container, good for development (hot-reload). tmpfs: in-memory storage, temporary data not persisted. Volumes are preferred for databases, bind mounts for development code, tmpfs for scratch data.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Docker layers and how does caching work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a layer. Docker caches each layer and reuses it on subsequent builds if the instruction and context haven't changed. Layer ordering matters: put infrequently-changing instructions first (package installs), then frequently-changing code last. Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from context. Cache invalidation: if a layer changes, all subsequent layers are rebuilt.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you secure a Docker container?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use minimal base images (Alpine, distroless), run as non-root user (USER directive), use read-only root filesystem, drop all capabilities then add needed ones (--cap-drop=ALL --cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE), use Docker Content Trust for image signing, scan images for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Snyk), use secrets management (Docker secrets, build secrets), set resource limits (memory, CPU), use security profiles (seccomp, AppArmor), enable user namespace remapping.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT.
interviewQuestions.answer: Both specify what command runs when container starts. ENTRYPOINT defines the executable (fixed). CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT or a default command if no ENTRYPOINT. docker run arguments override CMD but append to ENTRYPOINT. Pattern: ENTRYPOINT ['executable'] with CMD ['default_args']. This allows easy overriding: docker run image --custom-flag.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you handle Docker container logs?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker captures stdout/stderr from container processes. Logging drivers: json-file (default, written as JSON files), syslog, journald, gelf, fluentd, awslogs, splunk, etwlogs, gcplogs, logentries. Configure via --log-driver. For production: use centralized logging (ELK, Loki, Datadog). Never log to files inside containers — logs disappear with container. Use json-file with rotation (--log-opt max-size=10m --log-opt max-file=3).

interviewQuestions.question: How does Docker handle resource constraints?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker uses cgroups to limit resources per container: --memory (RAM limit), --memory-reservation (soft limit), --cpus (CPU limit, fractional), --cpuset-cpus (specific CPU cores), --pids-limit (max processes). Overcommitting: sum of limits can exceed host capacity. OOM killer: Docker kills container exceeding memory limit. Use docker stats to monitor. Set resource limits on all production containers.

interviewQuestions.question: What is Docker Swarm and how does it compare to Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker Swarm is Docker's native clustering and orchestration tool. Simpler setup, integrates with Docker CLI, uses YAML similar to Compose. Kubernetes has broader ecosystem, more features, better for complex deployments, larger community. Swarm is good for simpler deployments and teams already using Docker. K8s is better for large-scale, multi-cloud, complex orchestration needs.

books.title: Docker Deep Dive
books.author: Nigel Poulton
books.description: Zero to Docker in one book covering containers, images, Dockerfiles, Compose, Swarm, and production operations.

books.title: The Docker Book
books.author: James Turnbull
books.description: Comprehensive guide to Docker covering installation, containers, images, Dockerfiles, networking, and the Docker ecosystem.

books.title: Docker in Action
books.author: Jeff Nickoloff & Stephen Kuenzli
books.description: Practical Docker guide covering development workflows, CI/CD, orchestration, security, and production deployment.

books.title: Docker on Windows
books.author: Stefan Scherer & Mike G. Coleman
books.description: Comprehensive guide to running Docker on Windows including WSL2, Windows containers, and mixed-OS environments.

books.title: Kubernetes in Action
books.author: Marko Lukša
books.description: While primarily about K8s, the first chapters provide excellent context on containers and Docker before diving into orchestration.

books.title: Learn Docker - Fundamentals of Docker
books.author: Johan Classon
books.description: Hands-on guide covering Docker fundamentals through practical exercises and real-world scenarios.

bestPractices: Use specific base image tags (not 'latest') for reproducible builds
Prefer official Docker images for reliability and security
Use multi-stage builds to minimize final image size
Put frequently-changing instructions at the end of Dockerfile for better caching
Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from build context
Run containers as non-root user — add USER directive in Dockerfile
Use HEALTHCHECK to enable Docker to detect unresponsive containers
Use read-only root filesystem (--read-only) for production containers
Drop all capabilities and add only needed ones
Use Docker Compose for defining multi-service applications
Use named volumes for persistent data, bind mounts for development code
Set resource limits (--memory, --cpus) on all production containers
Use centralized logging — configure logging driver to ship logs
Don't store secrets in images — use Docker secrets or build secrets
Add HEALTHCHECK to Dockerfiles for service health awareness
Use liveness and readiness probes (especially in orchestration)
Prefer COPY over ADD (ADD has automatic tar extraction and URL features — non-obvious)
Combine RUN apt-get update && apt-get install in one layer
Use labels (LABEL) for metadata and organization
Scan images for vulnerabilities before deployment

careerOpportunities.description: Design and manage container infrastructure, CI/CD pipelines, and deployment automation.
Build internal developer platforms with Docker-based development environments and deployment workflows.
Ensure reliability and performance of containerized applications with monitoring, incident response, and automation.
Build and deploy containerized applications using Docker, microservices, and cloud-native patterns.
Advise organizations on Docker adoption, container strategy, CI/CD implementation, and DevOps transformation.
